results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (bearing a specific number) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by four Turkish nationals, the applicants, in late 1996.

The applicants were represented by their legal representative, a lawyer practising in a city in the country. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Convention institutions.

The applicants alleged that their case, which commenced in the late 1980s and terminated in the mid-1990s, was not heard within a reasonable time as required by the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the Court in late 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to the Second Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

In late 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed First Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision in mid-2003, the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicants and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

In autumn of 1980 the applicants were taken into police custody and were subsequently placed in detention on remand on suspicion of membership of an illegal organisation. A military public prosecutor filed a bill of indictment with a military martial law court charging the applicants under Article 146 of the Criminal Code with membership of an illegal organisation whose object was to undermine the constitutional order.

On various dates between the early and mid-1980s the applicants were released pending trial.

In late 1988 a military martial law court acquitted the applicants of the charges against them.

On various dates in spring and early summer of 1989 the applicants brought individual actions before a regional assize court against the Treasury, pursuant to Law no. 466. They requested compensation for their unjustified detention on remand.

In late 1989 the court decided to join the applicants’ cases.

In late 1993 a regional assize court awarded non‑pecuniary compensation to the applicants. The applicants appealed, arguing that the amount of compensation awarded was not sufficient.

In early 1995 a supreme appeals court quashed the judgment of the first instance court.

In mid-1995 a regional assize court increased the amount of non‑pecuniary compensation.

In mid-1995 the applicants again appealed.

In mid-1996 a supreme appeals court dismissed the applicants’ appeal and upheld the judgment of the assize court.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (referred to here as Application A and Application B) against the respondent State lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two nationals of the respondent State, Applicant A (whose application was lodged in mid-1998) and Applicant B (whose application was lodged later in 1998).

The Government of the respondent State (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, from the relevant government department.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property in a particular region were not dealt with within a reasonable time.

The applications were transmitted to the Court in late 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

In late 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Second Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision in late 2002 the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the meantime joined them in late 2000.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in an earlier year and a slightly earlier year respectively. Applicant A (the applicant in Application A) is a consultant psychiatrist; Applicant B (the applicant in Application B) is a medical secretary. They live in a certain city.

The applicants, together with Applicant A's spouse, were sued by “K”, a former patient of Applicant A, in respect of property in a particular region. The writ was issued in early 1986. Before the action came to trial, the plaintiff was adjudicated bankrupt in mid-1989 and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was set down to commence in the High Court in mid-1994 but was postponed until late in that same year because the judge was not available for the length of time needed for the hearing.

On an early day of trial, while K was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because K claimed to feel unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for his continued non-attendance, but nonetheless he remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by K’s non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded late that year and had to be adjourned until early the following year. Judgment was given in spring of that year.

The judge accepted the argument of the applicants and Applicant A's spouse that it was necessary to disregard K’s evidence because of his failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and Applicant A's spouse, the judge found that the transfer of the property in the particular region should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid K for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the doctor-patient relationship between Applicant A and K.

The formal record of the judge’s order was not prepared by the parties until late 1995. The applicants and Applicant A's spouse applied to the Court of Appeal for leave to appeal in early November of that year. The appeal bundles were lodged in late 1996. In early 1997 the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard in mid-April of that year, on which last date the Court of Appeal gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by Applicant A for the property, the Court of Appeal confirmed the decision of the trial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The trial judge had based his decision on the fact that Applicant A “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow [K] to use a specific unit in an initial period”. a specific Justice regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

A few days later in April of that year the applicants and Applicant A's spouse were refused leave by the Court of Appeal to appeal to the House of Lords. In late March the following year the applicants and Applicant A's spouse were refused leave to appeal by the House of Lords.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the respondent State lodged with a regional human rights body (“the Commission”) under a specific article of that convention of a prominent international human rights convention (“the Convention”) by nationals of the respondent State, the first applicant and the second applicant (“the applicants”), on 20 May 1998.

The applicants, who had been granted legal aid, were represented by a legal representative, a legal practitioner practising in Izmir. The respondent Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purpose of the proceedings before the Court.

On 21 March 2002 the Court decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of a relevant procedural article of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in the mid-1970s and the early 1970s respectively.

On 19 and 20 August 1995 respectively the applicants were taken into custody by police officers from the Anti-Terrorism Department of the Aydın Security Directorate on suspicion of their membership to a designated illegal organization.

In their police statements the applicants accepted the accusations against them.

On 22 August 1995 the applicants were brought before the investigating magistrate at the Aydın Magistrate’s Court, where they repeated the statements they had made to the police. The investigating magistrate ordered that they be placed in detention on remand.

In an indictment dated 6 September 1995, the prosecuting authority at the Izmir State Security Court initiated criminal proceedings against the applicants. He charged the first applicant with being member of an illegal armed group under Article 168 § 2 of the national Criminal Code and the second applicant with aiding and abetting an illegal armed group under Article 169 of the national Criminal Code.

During the hearings before the Izmir State Security Court, the applicants denied the statements they had made to the police and the investigating magistrate.

On 25 March 1997 the Izmir State Security Court, composed of two non-military judicial officers and a military judicial officer, convicted the applicants as charged and sentenced the first applicant to three years and nine months’ imprisonment and the second applicant to twelve years and six months’ imprisonment. Neither the applicants nor their legal practitioners were present in this last hearing.

The applicants appealed. On 12 November 1997 the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicants’ appeal, upholding the Izmir State Security Court’s reasoning and assessment of evidence. The decision, which was pronounced in the absence of the applicants and their legal practitioners, was deposited with the Registry of the Izmir State Security Court on 21 November 1997.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, the applicant, in late 2010.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practising in Lublin. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, the government's agent, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged, in particular, a breach of Article 3 of the Convention on account of the imposition on him of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime.

On a date in mid-2014 the application was communicated to the Government.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in a specific year in the late 1980s and is detained in a correctional facility in a particular region.

A. Criminal proceedings against the applicant

In late 2005 the applicant was arrested and remanded in custody. In the year following his arrest he was convicted of three counts of battery and robbery and received prison sentences ranging from two to four years. On a date in late November of the same year a regional court imposed a cumulative sentence for four criminal convictions.

B. Imposition of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime

On a date in early November 2010 a penitentiary commission at a correctional facility imposed on the applicant the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime. The commission held that the applicant had been the leader of or an active participant in a planned collective remonstrance in the correctional facility (Article 88a § 2 (2a) of the Code of Execution of Criminal Sentences), as the detainees refused to accept food provided by the prison authorities. The authorities learned that the detainees were planning another collective protest for a date in mid-November 2010. The commission also considered that the applicant was particularly depraved.

On a date in early 2011 the penitentiary commission at the correctional facility rejected an appeal lodged by the applicant and dismissed his request for leave to examine the appeal outside the statutory time-limit.

Subsequently, the applicant was transferred to a remand center in a particular region.

On a date in early February 2011 the penitentiary commission at the remand center reviewed and upheld the decision to apply the regime to the applicant, considering that he posed a serious threat to the security of the detention centre. The applicant appealed.

On a date in early April 2011 the regional court dismissed the appeal. The court referred to the reasons given in the decision from early November 2010, holding that the decision had been lawful and justified.

On a date in early March 2011 the applicant lodged a complaint with a regional inspectorate of prisons. He complained that he was having difficulties in accessing educational activities in the remand center. The authorities dismissed the complaint, holding that educational activities were not organised for dangerous prisoners.

On an unspecified date in the year 2011 the applicant notified the police of his allegedly unlawful classification as a “dangerous detainee” on a date in early November 2010. He claimed that the authorities of the correctional facility had exceeded their powers in imposing the regime on him. On a date in late April 2011 a district prosecutor's office refused to open an investigation in the case, holding that no offence had been committed.

On a date in late April 2011 the penitentiary commission at the remand center revoked its decision to classify the applicant as a “dangerous detainee”. It considered that the applicant’s behaviour had improved and that he no longer posed a threat to the security of the remand center.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [REDACTED]) against the United Kingdom lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a United Kingdom national, Mr [APPLICANT_NAME] (“the applicant”), in early 2000.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by the applicant's legal representative, a lawyer practising in a city in Scotland. The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, from a government department responsible for foreign affairs in the capital city.

The applicant alleged that his criminal proceedings exceeded a reasonable time contrary to Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

In mid-2001, the Court rejected part of the application as inadmissible.

In late 2001, the Court changed the composition of its sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Third Section.

By a final decision of early 2003, the Court declared the application partly admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The Chamber decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 3 in fine).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1964 and is currently serving a sentence of imprisonment in a correctional facility in northern Scotland.

The applicant was arrested on a charge of attempted murder arising out of a high speed car chase which occurred in the early hours of late October 1995. In late October 1995, he was arrested again, interviewed and cautioned in respect of a separate incident of rape and assault which also occurred during the early morning of late October 1995.

Shortly after these incidents in late 1995, the applicant appeared in court in respect of the various road traffic offences and the attempted murder of a police officer. In early November 1995, he was fully committed and remanded in custody on those charges (of which he was later acquitted).

In late November 1995, the applicant appeared before a magistrate and was committed in respect of the rape charge.

In mid-1996, the trial began in the High Court. In early July 1996, the applicant was convicted, by a majority verdict, of all charges and sentenced to 9 years 9 months' imprisonment.

In mid-July 1996, the applicant lodged an intention to appeal with the High Court sitting as an appeal court. In late August 1996, a Note of Appeal was lodged.

In late September 1996, a single judge refused the appeal. The applicant appealed.

In late October 1996, the Note of Appeal was allowed by three judges in restricted terms relating to the direction given by the trial judge on the special defence of alibi.

In late 1996, the appeal was set down to be heard. According to the Government, it did not take place as the applicant refused to leave prison. The applicant stated that contrary to statutory authority the prison governor refused to allow him to leave the prison wearing civilian clothes instead of a prison uniform for the hearing. A further hearing was fixed for mid-1997. On that date, the prosecution requested, and obtained, an adjournment as a prosecutor was engaged abroad.

In late 1997, the appeal was set down to be heard. It was discontinued due to lack of court time and pressure of other business. It was noted that the appeal was complex and would last half a day.

In mid-January 1998, there was another appeal hearing. As the applicant's solicitor had become aware that the prosecution had never cited a witness, to appear at the trial, he had lodged and argued an additional ground of appeal on this point. Following argument, he sought to lodge further additional grounds of appeal. The appeal was continued to mid-February 1998 to enable him to do so.

In mid-February 1998, the High Court held that the failure to hear the evidence of the witness resulted in unfairness and that further evidence should be heard. A designated judge was designated as the judge to hear the evidence. In late May 1998, he fixed a date to take the evidence of the witness. In late August 1998, the hearing was adjourned as the applicant's counsel withdrew from acting on his behalf.

In mid-October 1998, the witness gave evidence. In his note of late November 1998, the designated judge gave his view of the credibility and reliability of the witness. The appeal was relisted for hearing in early 1999 but the applicant's solicitors wrote to the court requesting that another date, possibly in the following week, be fixed due to the unavailability of counsel.

In mid-1999, the appeal was heard before the High Court.

In late July 1999, the judgment of the court was given by a senior judicial officer, another senior judge. It dismissed the applicant's appeal.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application with a specific number against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national from the region, the applicant, on a date in mid-1997.

The applicant was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practising in a city in the region. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, the Government's agent.

The applicant alleged that the Turkish occupation of the northern part of Cyprus had prevented him from having access to his home and properties.

The application was transmitted to the Court on a date in late 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

By a decision of a date in mid-1999 the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). In addition, third-party comments were received from the Government of Cyprus, which had exercised its right to intervene (Article 36 § 1 of the Convention and Rule 44 § 1 (b)).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the mid-20th century and lives in a city in the region.

The applicant claimed that until a period in the mid-1970s he used to live with his family in a house belonging to his parents in a particular village, a village in a specific district. In a period in the mid-1970s, as the Turkish army was advancing, the applicant and his family had been forced to leave the particular village. Ever since, they have not been able to return to the applicant’s parents’ house.

On a date in the early 1990s the applicant’s parents donated half of their house to the applicant. They also donated to him half of three other houses, two shops and a number of plots of land and water installations in the aforementioned village. The remaining half of the properties mentioned above was transferred to the applicant’s sister. The transfers at issue were recorded in the Land Registry of the Republic of Cyprus.

The applicant produced the relevant affirmations of ownership issued by the Republic of Cyprus. According to these documents, the applicant’s properties could be described as follows: (a) a specific plot of land (identified by a unique reference), an olive grove, area 2,282 sq. m; (b) two specific plots of land (identified by unique references), with areas of 3,862 sq. m and 3,405 sq. m respectively; (c) four specific plots of land (identified by unique references), with areas of 1,053 sq. m, 1,024 sq. m, 1,769 sq. m and 1,405 sq. m respectively; (d) two specific plots of land (identified by unique references), comprising lemon plantations, with areas of 5,219 sq. m and 1,469 sq. m respectively; (e) a specific plot of land (identified by a unique reference), a lemon plantation with bore hole, area: 4,877 sq. m; (f) several specific plots of land (identified by unique references), comprising a lemon plantation with two wells and one ground storey residence, area: 7,032 sq. m; (g) a specific plot of land (identified by a unique reference), a lemon plantation, area: 1,018 sq. m; (h) a specific plot of land (identified by a unique reference), containing a tank of an area of 37 sq. m with a freshwater spring; (i) multiple specific plots of land (identified by unique references), comprising a lemon plantation (area: 2,358 sq. m) with a bore hole and one tank (area: 84 sq. m); on this property had been constructed: a ground storey residence of an area of 152.25 sq. m; two shops of an area of 56 sq. m; two semidetached ground storey residences (areas: 152.25 sq. m and 63 sq. m respectively); one residence of an area of 219.41 sq. m; (j) several specific plots of land (identified by unique references), granting the right to use a freshwater spring.

The applicant alleged that the Turkish occupation of northern Cyprus had prevented him from using and/or enjoying his properties.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in a specific application number against the respondent State lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of the respondent State, the applicant, in November 2003.

The Government of the respondent State (“the Government”) were represented by a government representative from a government ministry.

In November 2005 the Court declared the application partly inadmissible and decided to communicate the complaint concerning the length of the administrative proceedings to the Government. Applying Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the mid-20th century and lives in a residential town.

In May 1996 the Tax Authority of a specific administrative region sent a preliminary consideration (övervägande) to the applicant informing him that it was considering disallowing the deduction he had made for the interest he had paid on debts in the amount of 2,000,000 units of local currency, as well as his declared capital gains for the sale of stocks in the amount of 775,000 units of local currency, that he had made in his tax return for the previous year. Further, it was considering imposing tax surcharges (skattetillägg) amounting to 147,000 units of local currency (approximately 15,800 units of international currency), i.e. 40% of the increased tax liability on the sum of 1,225,000 units of local currency.

The preliminary consideration was based on an investigation of a company which had done business with a substantial number of private persons. As concerned the applicant, it appeared that he had borrowed 40,000,000 units of local currency from the company and commissioned it to buy stocks for the same amount on his behalf. The applicant then sold the stocks back to the company at a fixed date, upon which he had made a capital gain. At the same time, he repaid the loan together with interest to the company. As the capital gain corresponded to the interest, they were set off against each other. In the Tax Authority’s view, the transactions had in reality never taken place and the applicant had simply received two statements of account and a promissory note in order to use them to obtain tax advantages.

The applicant was requested to submit any comments he might have to the Tax Authority by July 1996, which he did. He claimed that the transactions had taken place and that his tax return should be approved.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against the United Kingdom lodged with the Tribunal under Article 34 of the human rights Convention (“the Convention”) by a national of the United Kingdom, the applicant, on March 2002.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by the applicant's legal representative, a legal professional practising in London. The national government (“the government”) were represented by their Agent, from the government's foreign affairs department, London.

The applicant, serving a sentence of mandatory life imprisonment, alleged that the parole panel did not provide the guarantees and procedures required by Article 5 § 4 when examining whether he should be released and that he had no enforceable right to compensation for any breach of his rights as required by Article 5 § 5.

The application was allocated to a specific section of the Tribunal (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the designated judicial panel that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision of September 2003, the Tribunal declared the remainder of application admissible.

The applicant filed observations on just satisfaction and the national government replied (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in the late 1950s and is serving a sentence of life imprisonment in Correctional Facility A.

The applicant was convicted in or around the early 1980s for the murder of an acquaintance. He was sentenced to mandatory life imprisonment, and his minimum term (the minimum period of imprisonment required to satisfy the requirements of retribution and deterrence) was set at a specific number of years.

At the time of the applicant's latest parole panel review in the early 2000s, he was detained in Correctional Facility B as a “Category C” prisoner (prisoners being given a security category classification ranging from Category A (highest risk) to Category D (suitable for open conditions)). The parole panel recommended that the applicant should be transferred to open conditions, and that his detention should be reviewed after a period of two years. By letter dated July 2001 the ministerial authority informed the correctional services agency that he did not accept the parole panel's recommendation for transfer to open conditions, and that the next review should begin after a period of twelve months. In the letter to the applicant which accompanied the decision it was stated that: “... [t]he ministerial authority attaches particular weight to the psychologist's report, it has highlighted in some detail, the need for further exploration of your insight into, and responsibility for, the index offence and the apparent lack of empathy towards the victim.”

In June 2001 the applicant was transferred to Correctional Facility C. He claimed that he did not receive notification of the parole decision until sometime after mid-September 2001. The national government stated that a copy of the decision letter was sent to the applicant on July 2001 and a copy to the legal representatives then acting for the applicant on August 2001. When the applicant changed his legal representatives in September 2001, the authorities sent a copy of the decision to the new legal representatives.

The applicant stated that he wanted to seek judicial review of the decision, but was unable to find a legal representative who was prepared to act on his behalf. His then legal representatives gave advice that judicial review stood little prospect of success as the reports recommending a move to open conditions had been slightly ambiguous, there were a number of very unfavourable reports and the ministerial authority had given extensive reasons for rejecting the recommended move.

The applicant's first parole panel oral hearing under new arrangements was scheduled for November 2003. At the applicant's request, it was deferred until May 2004 to allow the applicant time to complete certain courses identified in the reports for the parole panel review.

results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (a specific case number) against a Nordic country lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a national of that country, the applicant, in early 1997.

The applicant was until mid-2000 represented by counsel and thereafter by another legal representative, both lawyers practising in a major city in the country. The national Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, from the Ministry for Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged, in particular, that his rights under Article 6 of the Convention had been violated on account of the lack of an oral hearing before the Administrative Court of Appeal in proceedings concerning social security benefits.

The application was transmitted to the Court in late 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to the First Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision of mid-2000 the Court declared the application partly admissible.

In late 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Fourth Section.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

In the autumn of 1986 the applicant, an individual of advanced age, applied for disability benefits under the Social Insurance Act (Lagen om allmän försäkring, 1962; hereinafter “the 1962 Act”). He claimed that he was in need of assistance and had additional costs due to a significant health issue requiring assistance resulting from a medical procedure several years prior.

By a decision of early 1987 the Social Insurance Office (försäkringskassan; hereinafter “the Office”) of a regional administrative division rejected the application, finding that the applicant’s needs or costs were not such as to make him eligible for disability benefits.

In late 1990 the applicant requested the Office to review its decision under chapter 20, section 10 a of the 1962 Act. He claimed that he had failed to fully state his need of assistance and his additional costs in the original application. In spring 1991 he asked the Office, alternatively, to consider his request as a new application and to grant him benefits as from late 1988.

In mid-1991 the Office found that there was no basis for a review of its earlier decision. Further, in regard to the applicant’s alternative claim, the Office noted that the applicant had reached the statutory retirement age at an earlier date and that information relating to the time that followed could not be taken into account under the relevant rules. Consequently, the applicant’s alternative claim was rejected.

The applicant appealed to the County Administrative Court (länsrätten) of the regional administrative division. In late 1992 the court gave judgment in his favour. It considered that the Office’s first decision had been based on insufficient information and that the Office should therefore have reviewed that decision at the applicant’s request. It noted that, according to the two medical certificates in the case, the applicant had considerable additional costs due to his health condition. Consequently, he was entitled to disability benefits as long as his need of assistance and his additional costs, taken together, attained the level required under the 1962 Act. Accepting the information submitted by the applicant in this regard, the court found that that level had been attained and granted him a disability allowance as from mid-1986. The court did not hold an oral hearing nor, apparently, did the applicant request one.

The National Social Insurance Board (Riksförsäkringsverket; hereinafter “the Board”) appealed against the County Administrative Court’s judgment to the Administrative Court of Appeal (kammarrätten) in a major city in the country, requesting that the Office’s decision of mid-1991 be confirmed. The Board claimed that the information in the case showed that, before reaching the statutory retirement age, the applicant had not suffered a functional impairment to such an extent that his need of assistance and extra expenses on account thereof had made him eligible for a disability allowance. Thus, the Office’s first decision could not be considered to have been incorrect due to it being based on incorrect or incomplete material. Consequently, there were no grounds for a review under the 1962 Act. The Board further requested the appellate court to stay the enforcement of the appealed judgment. The latter request was granted by a decision of late 1992.

The applicant made submissions in response to the Board’s appeal. In early 1993 he requested the Administrative Court of Appeal to hold an oral hearing in the case during which the person in charge of the case at the Office should give evidence relating to the meaning of its decision and the information on which it had been based. The applicant also asked the court to obtain the opinion of a medical expert.

In late 1993 the Administrative Court of Appeal rejected both requests. In regard to the request for an oral hearing, the court, after having restated section 9 of the Administrative Court Procedure Act (Förvaltningsprocesslagen, 1971; hereinafter “the 1971 Act”; see further paragraph 23 below), gave the following reasons: “Having regard to the subject-matter at issue and the information that has come to hand in the case, [the court] finds that an oral hearing is unnecessary and rejects the request to that effect. [The applicant] is invited to state the further circumstances he wishes to invoke and submit his final written observations in the case within two weeks after having been notified of this decision. The case can be determined notwithstanding a failure to submit such written observations.”

The applicant and the Board made further submissions. The applicant maintained that the Board had failed to adequately explain the reasons for its appeal and posed several questions to the Board. He requested that the court order the Board to clarify its position in the case. The court did not do so, however. The applicant’s submissions were, however, forwarded to the Board which replied that it maintained its position in the case. On account of the court’s refusal to order the Board to clarify its position and to hold a hearing, the applicant called into question the impartiality of the proceedings. By letters of late 1994 and early 1995 he reiterated his request for an oral hearing, stating that a hearing would give him the opportunity to ask questions to the Board and to present judgments in similar cases in support of his application for disability benefits. Furthermore, in assessing his condition, the court would benefit from having met him in person.

In late 1995 the Administrative Court of Appeal rejected the applicant’s renewed requests for an oral hearing and gave judgment in favour of the Board. Thus, by 3 votes to 2, it quashed the County Administrative Court’s judgment and confirmed the Office’s decision of mid-1991. Without giving any further reasons, it considered that there had been no basis for a review of the Office’s first decision. The dissenting judges considered that the applicant was entitled to the disability allowance granted to him by the County Administrative Court and that, for this reason, there were exceptional reasons to change the Office’s decision.

The applicant appealed to the Supreme Administrative Court (Regeringsrätten). He requested that the case be referred back to the Administrative Court of Appeal for re-examination or, alternatively, that the Supreme Administrative Court confirm the County Administrative Court’s judgment. He complained about the lack of an oral hearing in the Administrative Court of Appeal and also requested the Supreme Administrative Court to hold an oral hearing. In early 1997 the Supreme Administrative Court refused the applicant leave to appeal.

In late 1996 the applicant made a renewed request for a disability allowance. By a decision of late 1996 the Office found that he had particular needs and costs due to his health condition which made him eligible for disability benefits under the 1962 Act. As an allowance could be given retroactively only for the two years preceding the application, the applicant was granted an allowance as from late 1994.

By a decision of early 1987 the Social Insurance Office (försäkringskassan; hereinafter “the Office”) of a regional administrative division rejected the application, finding that the applicant’s needs or costs were not such as to make him eligible for disability benefits.

In late 1990 the applicant requested the Office to review its decision under chapter 20, section 10 a of the 1962 Act. He claimed that he had failed to fully state his need of assistance and his additional costs in the original application. In spring 1991 he asked the Office, alternatively, to consider his request as a new application and to grant him benefits as from late 1988.

In mid-1991 the Office found that there was no basis for a review of its earlier decision. Further, in regard to the applicant’s alternative claim, the Office noted that the applicant had reached the statutory retirement age at an earlier date and that information relating to the time that followed could not be taken into account under the relevant rules. Consequently, the applicant’s alternative claim was rejected.

The applicant appealed to the County Administrative Court (länsrätten) of the regional administrative division. In late 1992 the court gave judgment in his favour. It considered that the Office’s first decision had been based on insufficient information and that the Office should therefore have reviewed that decision at the applicant’s request. It noted that, according to the two medical certificates in the case, the applicant had considerable additional costs due to his health condition. Consequently, he was entitled to disability benefits as long as his need of assistance and his additional costs, taken together, attained the level required under the 1962 Act. Accepting the information submitted by the applicant in this regard, the court found that that level had been attained and granted him a disability allowance as from mid-1986. The court did not hold an oral hearing nor, apparently, did the applicant request one.

The National Social Insurance Board (Riksförsäkringsverket; hereinafter “the Board”) appealed against the County Administrative Court’s judgment to the Administrative Court of Appeal (kammarrätten) in a major city in the country, requesting that the Office’s decision of mid-1991 be confirmed. The Board claimed that the information in the case showed that, before reaching the statutory retirement age, the applicant had not suffered a functional impairment to such an extent that his need of assistance and extra expenses on account thereof had made him eligible for a disability allowance. Thus, the Office’s first decision could not be considered to have been incorrect due to it being based on incorrect or incomplete material. Consequently, there were no grounds for a review under the 1962 Act. The Board further requested the appellate court to stay the enforcement of the appealed judgment. The latter request was granted by a decision of late 1992.

The applicant made submissions in response to the Board’s appeal. In early 1993 he requested the Administrative Court of Appeal to hold an oral hearing in the case during which the person in charge of the case at the Office should give evidence relating to the meaning of its decision and the information on which it had been based. The applicant also asked the court to obtain the opinion of a medical expert.

In late 1993 the Administrative Court of Appeal rejected both requests. In regard to the request for an oral hearing, the court, after having restated section 9 of the Administrative Court Procedure Act (Förvaltningsprocesslagen, 1971; hereinafter “the 1971 Act”; see further paragraph 23 below), gave the following reasons: “Having regard to the subject-matter at issue and the information that has come to hand in the case, [the court] finds that an oral hearing is unnecessary and rejects the request to that effect. [The applicant] is invited to state the further circumstances he wishes to invoke and submit his final written observations in the case within two weeks after having been notified of this decision. The case can be determined notwithstanding a failure to submit such written observations.”

The applicant and the Board made further submissions. The applicant maintained that the Board had failed to adequately explain the reasons for its appeal and posed several questions to the Board. He requested that the court order the Board to clarify its position in the case. The court did not do so, however. The applicant’s submissions were, however, forwarded to the Board which replied that it maintained its position in the case. On account of the court’s refusal to order the Board to clarify its position and to hold a hearing, the applicant called into question the impartiality of the proceedings. By letters of late 1994 and early 1995 he reiterated his request for an oral hearing, stating that a hearing would give him the opportunity to ask questions to the Board and to present judgments in similar cases in support of his application for disability benefits. Furthermore, in assessing his condition, the court would benefit from having met him in person.

In late 1995 the Administrative Court of Appeal rejected the applicant’s renewed requests for an oral hearing and gave judgment in favour of the Board. Thus, by 3 votes to 2, it quashed the County Administrative Court’s judgment and confirmed the Office’s decision of mid-1991. Without giving any further reasons, it considered that there had been no basis for a review of the Office’s first decision. The dissenting judges considered that the applicant was entitled to the disability allowance granted to him by the County Administrative Court and that, for this reason, there were exceptional reasons to change the Office’s decision.

The applicant appealed to the Supreme Administrative Court (Regeringsrätten). He requested that the case be referred back to the Administrative Court of Appeal for re-examination or, alternatively, that the Supreme Administrative Court confirm the County Administrative Court’s judgment. He complained about the lack of an oral hearing in the Administrative Court of Appeal and also requested the Supreme Administrative Court to hold an oral hearing. In early 1997 the Supreme Administrative Court refused the applicant leave to appeal.

In late 1996 the applicant made a renewed request for a disability allowance. By a decision of late 1996 the Office found that he had particular needs and costs due to his health condition which made him eligible for disability benefits under the 1962 Act. As an allowance could be given retroactively only for the two years preceding the application, the applicant was granted an allowance as from late 1994.

results.judged_text:
The case was referred to the Court on a specific date in mid-1993 by the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission“) and on early August of the same year by the Government of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland (“the Government“), within the three-month period laid down by Article 32 para. 1 and Article 47 (art. 32-1, art. 47) of the Convention. It originated in a specific application number against the United Kingdom lodged with the Commission under Article 25 (art. 25) on an early date in 1991 by a citizen of the respondent State, the applicant. The Commission’s request referred to Articles 44 and 48 (art. 44, art. 48) and to the declaration whereby the United Kingdom recognised the compulsory jurisdiction of the Court (Article 46) (art. 46); the Government’s application referred to Article 48 (art. 48). The object of the request and the application was to obtain a decision as to whether the facts of the case disclosed a breach by the respondent State of its obligations under Article 6 para. 3 (c) (art. 6-3-c).

In response to the enquiry made in accordance with Rule 33 para. 3 (d) of Rules of Court A, the applicant stated that he wished to take part in the proceedings and designated the lawyer who would represent him (Rule 30).

On a later date in August 1993, the President of the Court decided that in the interests of the proper administration of justice this case and the case of Maxwell v. the United Kingdom (another related case number) should be heard by the same Chamber (Rule 21 para. 6). The Chamber to be constituted included ex officio the elected judge of the respondent State's nationality, and the President of the Court (Rule 21 para. 3 (b)). On a few days later in August 1993, in the presence of the Registrar, the President drew by lot the names of the other seven members, namely several judges (Article 43 in fine of the Convention and Rule 21 para. 4) (art. 43).

As President of the Chamber (Rule 21 para. 5), the President of the Court, acting through the Registrar, consulted the Agent of the Government, the applicant’s lawyer and the Delegate of the Commission on the organisation of the proceedings (Rules 37 para. 1 and 38). Pursuant to the order made in consequence, the Registrar received the applicant’s memorial on late in 1993 and the Government’s memorial on early in the following year. On early April of that year the Government submitted their written reply to the applicant’s claims for just satisfaction under Article 50 (art. 50) of the Convention. On mid-April of that year the Secretary to the Commission informed the Registrar that the Delegate would not submit written observations in reply.

In accordance with the President’s decision, the hearing took place in public in the venue where the human rights court proceedings took place, on a few days later in April of that year. The Court had held a preparatory meeting beforehand. There appeared before the Court:

for the Government the Agent for the Government, Foreign and Commonwealth Office, Agent, the Lord Advocate, QC, Lord Advocate, a counsel for the Government, Counsel, advisers for the Government;

for the Commission the Delegate for the Commission, Delegate;

for the applicant a counsel for the applicant, QC, Counsel, a solicitor for the applicant, Solicitor. The Court heard addresses by the Delegate for the Commission, the Lord Advocate and a counsel for the applicant as well as replies to its questions.

AS TO THE FACTS

I. CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant is a citizen of the respondent State and an adult. He lives in a town in a region of the United Kingdom. On a date in late 1989 three masked men, armed with a shotgun and a knife, carried out a robbery at a post office in a specific village in a region of the United Kingdom, in the course of which they assaulted three post-office employees. They also caused damage to a motor car. Following investigations, the applicant and two other men were arrested and remanded in custody. The applicant was indicted on a charge of assault and armed robbery, a charge of wilful damage and three charges relating to firearms. Between late March and early April 1990 he stood trial in the High Court of Justiciary sitting in a major city in the country.

The applicant received legal aid for the preparation of his defence and for his representation by counsel at the trial.

During the trial a prosecution witness, the witness, entered the courtroom prior to giving evidence and spoke to one of the applicant’s co-accused, against whom charges had been dropped. When the witness was called to give evidence, the applicant’s counsel objected on the basis of her earlier presence in court. The trial judge adjourned the case and instructed prosecuting counsel to make further inquiries into the matter so that it could be determined whether the witness’s attendance was due to any negligence or failure on the part of the Crown. Prosecuting counsel informed the judge that inquiries had revealed no fault on the part of anyone for whom the Crown bore responsibility. This was not disputed by the applicant’s counsel, who, moreover, was unable to give any indication as to how the witness’s evidence could be affected by her earlier presence in court. The trial judge accordingly took the view that no injustice would be done by the witness’s examination. Finding that the relevant legal conditions were satisfied (see paragraph 17 below), he therefore exercised his discretion and permitted her to give evidence. Subsequently, counsel for the applicant had the opportunity to cross-examine the witness on the matter of her attendance in court. In her evidence, the witness stated that the applicant had been at her home the evening before the robbery and had spoken of robbing the post office. The following morning he had left the house and returned later with a bag, in a state of nervousness. Other evidence against the applicant included the fact that a shotgun and various items stolen from the post office were found on the applicant’s premises. In addition, a different witness identified the applicant as being one of the robbers and the witness’s statement was supported by her young daughter.

The jury found the applicant guilty on all the charges. Taking account of his numerous previous convictions, the judge sentenced him to eight years’ imprisonment.

On mid-April 1990 the applicant, through his solicitor, lodged an intimation of intention to appeal against conviction.

The applicant instructed new solicitors, who asked a different counsel to advise on the prospects of an appeal and to draft a note of appeal. In his opinion dated early June 1990 counsel stated his view that the only possible ground of appeal related to the “admissibility of the evidence of the witness“ and to the question “that the trial judge erred in exercising discretion in allowing her to be present“. However, he conceded that he had not been provided with sufficient information to be able properly to base an opinion or to frame grounds of appeal. Notwithstanding this acknowledgement he did draft a note of appeal which contained six grounds, the first two of which concerned the trial judge’s decision to admit the witness. This note of appeal was filed by the applicant’s solicitors on a few days later in June 1990.

Up to this point all the work had been covered by the legal aid granted for the trial. An application to extend legal aid to the appeal proceedings had been submitted in May 1990. On late July 1990 the Scottish Legal Aid Board (“the Board“) asked the applicant to provide an opinion from counsel on the prospects of success. The earlier opinion dated early June 1990 was accordingly forwarded to the Board, together with a supplementary opinion from the same counsel dated early September 1990. In this latter opinion counsel reiterated the view that the appeal rested on the question whether the witness’s evidence was admissible. However, he again stated that he had not been provided with sufficient material on which to assess the merits of the appeal. “I regret meantime I am unable to answer the fundamental question in this case“, he concluded.

On late September 1990 the applicant’s solicitors informed the Board by telephone that counsel had finally decided that he could not support the application for legal aid or the appeal; that they were of the same view; and that, consequently, they could no longer act for the applicant. The terms of that telephone call were confirmed in a letter dated early November 1990 from the solicitors to the Board.

On mid-November 1990 the Scottish Legal Aid Board notified the applicant’s solicitors that his application for legal aid had been refused as the Board was not satisfied, as it was required to be under the relevant legislation (see paragraph 26 below), that he had substantial grounds for taking an appeal and that it was reasonable that he should receive legal aid. In a letter of late December 1990 to the applicant the Board added that it could not see any merit in the appeal. Despite the advice of his solicitors and counsel, the applicant decided to proceed with the appeal.

The applicant, who had no legal knowledge and had no assistance with his submissions, presented his own case on late January 1991 before the High Court of Justiciary, sitting in a major city in the country and acting as an appellate court. The Crown was represented by counsel. There is no indication in the record that the court required counsel for the Crown to address it.

The appeal court considered the first two grounds of appeal relating to the witness and held that these were ill-founded and that the trial judge had approached the matter properly. As the applicant did not address the court on the remaining grounds of appeal, these were not considered by the court. It did however examine other points raised by the applicant, but reached “the clear conclusion that there was no miscarriage of justice in the case“. The appeal against conviction was unanimously dismissed.
